_doc: Live-narration ADAPTATION cue sheet for The Unnecessary, Ch2 Scene 1 (Inventory of What Still Works). Lena's viewpoint, the clinic in the afternoon before the midnight authentication deadline. Mostly solitary-interior: the narrator (narrator voice, clean, calm) carries Lena's third-person perception and interior verbatim to canon (the only trims are the physical beats the SFX legitimately replace -- the floorboard taking weight in the doorway, the front door going). Dramatized voice elements: (1) the manufacturer NOTICE sentence ('Remote authentication...') in the flat machine 'notice' voice via the book-scope 'notice' filter, framed by the narrator's 'a sentence built so that no human voice could be heard inside it'; (2) the Tomas/Lena ward exchange performed in their own voices (tomas_herrera newly uploaded; lena_okafor), 'he said'/'Tomas said' tags cut because the voices carry them, the narration ABOUT Tomas ('the only other nurse she fully trusted...') kept as the narrator's; (3) the scene's final beat -- Eli, heard for the first time CLEAN (filter none, low/dry) after the degraded link of Ch1, says her name in the room. SFX: a single floorboard creak (replaces 'A floorboard took weight in the doorway'), a subtle diagnostic-scanner panel-wake, the clinic front door + cold draft. 'text' = canon spelling; 'tts' = pronunciation-corrected line actually sent (past-tense read->red, noun read->reed, 23:59->twenty-three fifty-nine, Adeyemi/Okonkwo/Priya/Vesely phoneticized). Reveal-safe: no Morrow, no buried drive, no six-years secret, no statement of what midnight does; Eli's coming reads only as repair/forgery. Voices: narrator, notice(narrator timbre), lena_okafor, tomas_herrera, eli_rook. The respiratory controller is IDLE through the day (cool casing, nothing under her palm) -- deliberately NO vent-cycle SFX.

filters._doc: No scene-special filters. 'notice' resolves up-scope from the book filters.json (flat machine-composed manufacturer notice). 'none' is provided by the mixer.

tuning._doc: Narrator stays calm/measured regardless of scene intensity (over-expressive narration artifacts). Characters carry the scene's banked fury and dry steadiness. 'notice' falls back to the narrator voice id, tuned flat/institutional. Per-cue overrides win; short clipped lines get lower temperature + higher cfg_weight to avoid garble.

cues.text: The card was what Lena looked for first when she came into the room now, before the man in the bed, before the numbers.

It was an index card, white going soft at the corners, taped to the housing of the respiratory controller over the place where a small status light used to burn. The light had meant the machine was certified, current, blessed by the company that built it. The company was gone. The light was dead. So she had written what she needed to know on a card and taped it where the light had been.
Cycle good. Last clean read, and a time. Pressures, in her own hand. The card told her what the light no longer could: the machine had cycled clean the last time it ran, with no promise of the next.
cues.tts: Cycle good. Last clean reed, and a time. Pressures, in her own hand. The card told her what the light no longer could: the machine had cycled clean the last time it ran, with no promise of the next.

cues.text: She put her hand flat on the casing.
The casing was cool under her hand, and still, the machine idle on its stand through the daylight while the man in the bed did his own breathing.

Mr. Adeyemi was awake. Sixty-one years old, narrow under the blanket, propped up, managing his own slow lungs for as long as the afternoon would let him. In the day he came off it for hours at a stretch, sat up, complained about the food, told her about a daughter in a protected zone who could not get out to see him and would not have been let back in if she had.
cues.tts: Mr. Ah-deh-yeh-mee was awake. Sixty-one years old, narrow under the blanket, propped up, managing his own slow lungs for as long as the afternoon would let him. In the day he came off it for hours at a stretch, sat up, complained about the food, told her about a daughter in a protected zone who could not get out to see him and would not have been let back in if she had.

cues.text: At night it went the other way. His chest could not hold the count alone, and the machine took it up, a small even push and release for lungs that would not make it through the dark. The daughter went quiet in him then, along with everything else, and Lena watched the card. That was tonight, still ahead of them both. For now the casing stayed cool, the man stayed talking, and the controller waited with her for the dark.
She made herself take her hand off the casing and look at her phone.

Nine and a half hours. The notice had given a time, as they always gave a time, and the time was 23:59, their polite way of saying midnight without writing the word, set in the same clean type as the cellular and power notices, a sentence built so that no human voice could be heard inside it.
cues.tts: Nine and a half hours. The notice had given a time, as they always gave a time, and the time was twenty-three fifty-nine, their polite way of saying midnight without writing the word, set in the same clean type as the cellular and power notices, a sentence built so that no human voice could be heard inside it.

cues.text: Remote authentication for the listed devices will be discontinued effective end of day, October the third.

She had read it to Eli that afternoon over a link that dropped every third word. She had heard him on the other end go up into the place she could never follow, and told him to come down out of it and stay with the body. He had said he would come. He was coming. That helped, but not as much as she needed, and she would not let herself pretend otherwise until she had counted the rest of what she had.
cues.tts: She had red it to Eli that afternoon over a link that dropped every third word. She had heard him on the other end go up into the place she could never follow, and told him to come down out of it and stay with the body. He had said he would come. He was coming. That helped, but not as much as she needed, and she would not let herself pretend otherwise until she had counted the rest of what she had.

cues.text: She went to count it.

The clinic was three patient rooms, a ward of six beds, an exam room, the front room with its bench and its counter, and a back hall that ran past a supply closet to a door she had not opened in a year.

She had taken it over from a dentist who had taken it over from an insurance office, and the bones of all three were still in it if you looked, the reception window, the little frosted partitions, the carpet glue under the new flooring.

At this hour the place ran on daylight. She kept the electric lights off to spare the batteries, and the rooms took what the afternoon gave them through the windows, a thin used-up light that thinned further the deeper in you went and did not reach the inner rooms at all. The cold had come into the building as it came into everything now that warmth was rationed out to them, settling into the floor and rising through the soles of her shoes.
In the second room she stopped at the diagnostic scanner.

It stood against the wall, white and unmarked and perfect, a clean curved shell with a bed that slid into it, and it was the best doctor in the building and they both knew it. She had watched it find a tumor in a woman's pancreas that Lena's own hands and eyes and forty-six years would have missed for another two months, and those two months would have been the difference. It was better than her at everything but the one task that mattered tonight, which was running.
She woke its panel.

It lit and asked her, in its mild way, to wait while it confirmed its license, and the little wheel turned while it reached out to ask whether it was still allowed to know what it knew. Tonight the server answered. The wheel stopped. The machine said it was ready.

At midnight the server would stop answering. The next time someone pressed this panel the wheel would turn and turn and never stop, and the machine would sit here, white and unmarked and perfect, knowing everything and permitted to say none of it.
She put it to sleep before it could finish offering to scan her.

In the same room, the medication-management unit, a gray cabinet the size of a small refrigerator with a screen and a row of locked drawers. It held the doses, decided how much and how often, would not let her hand more morphine than a body should have because it knew the body and she, in a tired hour, might forget. She loved it for that on her good days and resented it on the rest.

It had the same heart as the scanner and the controller, the same need to phone a company to be allowed to do its work, and the company had written the same sentence about it. After midnight it might keep the schedules already loaded and refuse to take a new one. It might lock the drawers and keep the keys. She did not know which. No one knew which.
The not-knowing was the trap of it: she was being asked to plan a night around three machines whose behavior after one specific minute no living person could tell her.
She did not turn around.
Priya wants to know about the Caldwell girl.
cues.tts: Pree-yah wants to know about the Caldwell girl.

cues.text: He was the only other nurse she fully trusted tonight, thirty, steady, one she had not had to teach to be afraid quietly.
Whether we keep her or send her home. And the Okonkwos brought their thing. It's on the counter.
cues.tts: Whether we keep her or send her home. And the Oh-konk-wohs brought their thing. It's on the counter.

cues.text: What thing.
Eggs.
Dozen, looks like. For the father's visit Tuesday. Mrs. Okonkwo said to tell you they're from the Vesely place, the brown ones, she said you'd want the brown ones.
cues.tts: Dozen, looks like. For the father's visit Tuesday. Missus Oh-konk-woh said to tell you they're from the Vess-eh-lee place, the brown ones, she said you'd want the brown ones.

cues.text: He said it without weight, a total read off a register, because that was the total now. Eggs.
cues.tts: He said it without weight, a total reed off a register, because that was the total now. Eggs.

cues.text: I said thank you. I didn't know what else to do with a fee.

There was nothing to enter the eggs into. There had been a system once, a screen at the counter that took a card and sent a number to an office that sent a bill to a company that sent the company's money back.

The screen was still there, dark. The office was gone. The company had folded into another company that had itself been discontinued, and so a dozen brown eggs sat on the counter where the screen used to be, the entire economy now, start to finish, the Vesely place to the Okonkwos to her.
cues.tts: The screen was still there, dark. The office was gone. The company had folded into another company that had itself been discontinued, and so a dozen brown eggs sat on the counter where the screen used to be, the entire economy now, start to finish, the Vess-eh-lee place to the Oh-konk-wohs to her.

cues.text: The Caldwell girl. What's her fever doing.
Down. Hundred even, last hour.
Then she's mine, not the machine's. Keep her. I'm not sending a child into the cold on a night I can't promise her the scanner if she spikes.
The words came out and she caught their shape on the way, the scanner she could not promise.
Tell Priya to watch her by hand. Temperature every two hours, the old way, write it down. If she goes up, come get me, don't go to the cabinet, come get me.
cues.tts: Tell Pree-yah to watch her by hand. Temperature every two hours, the old way, write it down. If she goes up, come get me, don't go to the cabinet, come get me.

cues.text: Because the cabinet might lock.
Not a question. He had read the same notice on her face all afternoon.
cues.tts: Not a question. He had red the same notice on her face all afternoon.

cues.text: Because I want to be the one deciding the dose tonight. Not the cabinet, and not whatever the cabinet turns into at midnight.
He went. She stood a moment in the dim room with the two clean machines reading their perfect numbers at no one, and did the math she hated, the kind that came out in people.
Mr. Adeyemi on the controller, a whole night. The Caldwell child, fever falling, hers by hand. Two on the ward who needed nothing the machines gave.
cues.tts: Mr. Ah-deh-yeh-mee on the controller, a whole night. The Caldwell child, fever falling, hers by hand. Two on the ward who needed nothing the machines gave.

cues.text: A man coming in the morning who would need the scanner and would have to be told to drive to a clinic two towns over that might or might not still have one running, that might or might not ask him for papers he did not have. Equipment she could not trust, judgment she could.
To sort the ones who could wait from the ones who could not, she had to know which was which, and the machines that were supposed to tell her had been told to stop at a minute she had not picked.
She caught herself standing too long.

She drifted back to the controller without deciding to, to the cool idle housing and her flat hand on it and nothing moving under her palm, as if presence were a plan. It was not. A hand on a casing authorized nothing.
She made herself move. Standing still was what you did when there was nothing left to do, and there was a great deal left to do, and she was not at the end of it yet.
She was at the counter, looking at the eggs without seeing them,
and a cold draft reached her down the hall. A voice she had only ever heard through a thinning link, in a room now instead of out of a speaker, low and dry and whole.
Lena.
She left the eggs and went to meet him.
